theorem_statement:
Matrix determinant lemma.

Let \(A\) be an invertible \(n\times n\) matrix over \(\mathbb{R}\) (or over any commutative field), and let \(u,v\in \mathbb{R}^n\) be column vectors. Then
\[
\det(A+uv^{T})=\det(A)\bigl(1+v^{T}A^{-1}u\bigr).
\]

Equivalently, if one first proves the special case
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})=1+v^{T}u,
\]
then the general statement follows immediately by factoring
\[
A+uv^{T}=A\bigl(I+A^{-1}uv^{T}\bigr)=A\bigl(I+(A^{-1}u)v^{T}\bigr).
\]

This is the classical matrix determinant lemma for a rank-one update. It is a concrete finite-dimensional algebraic identity that frequently appears inside larger arguments in matrix analysis, numerical linear algebra, random matrix theory, optimization, and perturbation theory.
raw_proof_text:
We prove the matrix determinant lemma
\[
\det(A+uv^{T})=\det(A)\bigl(1+v^{T}A^{-1}u\bigr)
\]
for an invertible \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\) and column vectors \(u,v\in\mathbb{R}^n\).

The proof is best organized in two stages.

## Stage 1: the special case \(A=I\)

We first prove the identity
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})=1+v^{T}u.
\]
This is the essential finite-dimensional algebraic core.

Write the rank-one matrix \(uv^{T}\) in columns. Its \(j\)-th column is \(v_j u\), where \(v_j\) is the \(j\)-th coordinate of \(v\). Therefore the \(j\)-th column of \(I+uv^{T}\) is
\[
e_j+v_j u,
\]
where \(e_j\) is the \(j\)-th standard basis vector.

Hence
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})
=
\det(e_1+v_1u,\dots,e_n+v_nu).
\]
Now use the multilinearity of the determinant in the columns. Expanding this determinant produces a sum of terms obtained by choosing from each column either the \(e_j\) part or the \(v_j u\) part.

A key observation is that any term in which we choose the \(u\)-part from two or more columns is zero. Indeed, such a term has at least two columns proportional to \(u\), so those columns are linearly dependent, and the determinant vanishes.

Therefore only the following terms survive:

1. the term in which we choose every \(e_j\), namely
\[
\det(e_1,\dots,e_n)=1;
\]

2. the terms in which we choose the \(u\)-part in exactly one column, say the \(j\)-th column, and choose \(e_i\) in all other columns.

Thus
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})
=
1+
\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j\det(e_1,\dots,e_{j-1},u,e_{j+1},\dots,e_n).
\]
It remains to evaluate the determinant
\[
\det(e_1,\dots,e_{j-1},u,e_{j+1},\dots,e_n).
\]
This is exactly the \(j\)-th coordinate \(u_j\) of \(u\). One way to see this is to expand along the \(j\)-th column in the standard basis, or simply note that replacing the \(j\)-th standard basis vector by \(u\) in the identity matrix produces a matrix whose determinant is \(u_j\).

Hence
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})
=
1+
\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j u_j
=
1+v^{T}u.
\]
This proves the special case.

## Stage 2: deduction of the general formula

Now let \(A\) be invertible. Since \(A\) is invertible, we may factor
\[
A+uv^{T}=A\bigl(I+A^{-1}uv^{T}\bigr).
\]
If we define
\[
w:=A^{-1}u,
\]
then this becomes
\[
A+uv^{T}=A(I+w v^{T}).
\]
Taking determinants and using multiplicativity of the determinant gives
\[
\det(A+uv^{T})=\det(A)\det(I+w v^{T}).
\]
By the special case already proved,
\[
\det(I+w v^{T})=1+v^{T}w.
\]
Substituting \(w=A^{-1}u\), we obtain
\[
\det(I+w v^{T})=1+v^{T}A^{-1}u.
\]
Therefore
\[
\det(A+uv^{T})=\det(A)\bigl(1+v^{T}A^{-1}u\bigr).
\]
This is exactly the matrix determinant lemma.

## Remarks on why this is a good Formal Islands benchmark

This theorem is a strong Formal Islands target because the surrounding mathematical context can be much broaderŌĆöfor example, a larger proof involving perturbation theory, ShermanŌĆōMorrison-type updates, or random matrix argumentsŌĆöwhile the formal island itself is a completely rigid finite-dimensional identity.

The most natural local node to formalize is the special-case identity
\[
\det(I+uv^{T})=1+v^{T}u.
\]
That node is already nontrivial and mathematically meaningful. It is also very well suited to Lean: the statement lives entirely in finite-dimensional matrix algebra, with explicit determinants, dot products, and rank-one matrices. There is essentially no temptation to drift into arbitrary measure spaces, Hilbert spaces, or other abstract ambient settings.

A secondary possible node is the factorization step
\[
\det(A+uv^{T})=\det(A)\det(I+A^{-1}uv^{T}),
\]
but the best single core island is the exact identity for \(I+uv^{T}\), since once that is certified, the full theorem follows immediately by one short algebraic reduction.

This completes the proof.
